## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right, y'all. So today, obviously, is about scale. And what's fun about this topic is that it is definitely the easiest part of media buying. When it comes to tactics and how difficult something is and how long it takes to do something, the scaling part of media buying is actually the easiest. But the issue, maybe not the issue, the misconception which we'll get to here in a minute, is that like if I asked most of you guys, when you guys filled out the intake forms for this class, most of you said that your issue was with scale, which makes sense. I mean, it could be your issue because scale is basically doing more of what's working or really growing your account. But what's funny, whenever I work with companies one-on-one or whenever I dive into your old businesses, I realize that what's actually holding you back from scale are the fundamental things that we've really covered in the first seven weeks of this class and the ability to repeat that process for different avatars and to come up with different hooks and to come up with new products in your business to grow your product line. Most of scale really comes down to the mastery of the process that we learned the first seven weeks of this class. There really isn't a lot of tactical hacks or tips or tricks that you guys are missing out on when it comes to scale other than really nailing everything that we taught in the first seven to eight weeks. So with media buying, scaling is actually really easy because it means that you have achieved some level of success that you are ready to scale it. That is usually where people fall off, is that they've either missed something in those core elements of the campaign. They're copies off, they're creatives off, they're offers off, or they've just decided to quit. So maybe their first few test campaigns haven't worked and they're having trouble going through the troubleshooting process like we talked about last week. Maybe they're not sure exactly what's wrong with their campaign and they don't really make it to the point where they achieve proof of concept so that they are actually ready to scale. And that's just an interesting point that I want to make because I think a lot of people are stuck in that part of the process. It's not really the tactical action of scaling that they're having trouble with because that's really easy. It's actually pretty simple. And you're overlooking the importance and really how difficult nailing the campaign creation process,

## [03:01 - 06:02]

how difficult that can be. So when we talk about scale, there are lots of tactics that I am going to share today. I'm going to show you my process for scaling. But when you think about it, this actually is the easiest part of this process because we've gotten something to work and we're ready to do more with it. So just a different way of thinking about this than you usually hear at conferences or on podcasts. It seems like most people get stuck in the scaling phase, but really they're stuck in the proof of concept phase. So just a few misconceptions about scaling. The first is that most people assume that your return on ad spend or CPA or cost per lead, whatever that success metric is that you're measuring, that it will stay the same as you scale. So I'll work with students or clients who maybe they generated a thousand leads or a thousand purchases and their cost per acquisition was five bucks or their return on ad spend was a five X return on ad spend. And things are looking really good and they want to scale. They have proof of concept and they're ready to scale. So they start upping the budget, they start targeting new audiences, they start the process of scaling that we'll discuss further today. And then a week or so later they'll say, okay, Molly, you know, I've, I'm up to 5,000 purchases now, but my cost per acquisition is 10 bucks and my return on ad spend has gone down to 2.5. And this is terrible. What am I going to do? Why is this happening? And they don't realize that 99% of the time that is how scaling works, right? That is really how these paid traffic platforms function. The more you spend and the more that you saturate the audience that you're targeting and really the longer that your campaign fatigues and starts to drop down in ranking when it comes to Facebook's auction. So your cost per click starts to go up and of course your CPA goes up, your return on ad spend decreases. That is a natural part of media buying on platforms like Facebook, Google and YouTube. And yes, there are some things that we can do to combat that, like constantly launching new campaigns based off of insights from campaigns that we're currently running, hoping that those new campaigns can rig higher in the auction and start to produce numbers that look a little bit more reasonable for us. But within that one campaign that I was just discussing that, that example that I was using, it is very natural that over time, you know, your things are going to start to fatigue and your return on ad spend is going to go down and that your cost per acquisition and your cost

## [06:02 - 09:03]

per lead is going to climb. Very few times have I seen inside of a campaign after about the week or two week mark, after I've done any troubleshooting or optimization that I feel that I can do, very rarely do I see after the two week mark that the CPA actually starts to decrease or that the return on ad spend starts to increase. After that, your campaign is simply going to start to fatigue and that's okay. That's when you should start to think about new campaigns that you can launch to help overcome this. But I just want to cover this misconception because a lot of people would assume that they can scale from a thousand leads a day to five thousand leads a day, but maintain that five dollar CPA or maintain that five X return on ad spend. And yes, there are things you can do to help prolong that fatigue and we'll talk about that today. But this is just natural. This is just how paid traffic platforms function and it's nothing for you to get frustrated about or create negative energy about. It's just really how it works. And if you don't understand this, you're going to face a lot of frustration and probably think that you're doing things wrong along the process. And this is why a lot of you tell me, Molly, I don't know how to scale. I'm not scaling correctly. As soon as I start to increase my budget, where as soon as time passes, things just start to get more expensive. And that's okay. It's not just you. It's all of us. Like we just, you know, we had been essentially like making a small return. I think on every campaign we ran before we made this decision. And then we switched it. ## [15:11 - 18:16]

new lead magnets and webinars. And I know we created a whole new avatar of agency owners and students that we wanted to go after. And we went deep into way different types of creatives and copy and targeting. That is really where the scale came from. It wasn't that I learned a new button to click in Facebook. Yeah, an interesting note on that is just something that was working really well before we did this was we had pretty hyper-targeted offers. So we were doing something that was designed specifically for media buyers, something that was specifically designed for content marketers or email writers, copywriters. But to go to a bigger pond to reach a broader audience, we had to create some offers that were a lot more generic marketing, generic business ones. And they didn't convert as well. If you send somebody who's a Facebook ad buyer that lives in Kentucky to a landing page where it says this is the one secret to successfully buying Facebook ads in Kentucky, that person is going to convert it like 90% on that page. It's so targeted. It's so perfect for them. But there's probably not more than 1,000 or so people in Kentucky doing that. So your ability to scale is actually pretty limited because your pool of audience is pretty small. So you change it to media buyers in the U.S. Suddenly the pool is much, much larger. You're going to be driving a lot more people, but it's not quite as hyper-targeted. So they're going to convert at a slightly lower rate. But I would be much happier with 100 customers at a two return on ad spend. So don't get so caught up in campaigns that have a

## [21:22 - 24:27]

super high return on ad spend, but don't have a high volume of results because the high volume of results, whether they're leads or customers for your business, that's where true scale comes from as long as you're still cash flow positive. So scale isn't just about return on ad spend. And it's why when you guys see me in ad accounts, especially at the ad level, a lot of times I'm sorting ads or ad sets by number of results. And of course, I'm looking at the CPA or CPL and I'm looking at the return on ad spend, but I'm mainly looking at the volume of results generated because that's what's important to me. I need that volume for scale, whether it's number of people on my webinar, whether it's number of purchases, whatever I'm doing, that volume is more important than the return on ad spends. And it's something that we should definitely keep in mind. So just to touch a little bit on last week, so optimization is really about fixing a campaign. So what's not working about a campaign? How can we fix it? Scale is really about getting more results from something that's already working. And the optimization process has two parts, maintenance and troubleshooting. And I talked a lot about troubleshooting last week. But the optimization process really begins with the maintenance. So this is the day to day troubleshooting is more of a reaction. So I just launched a test campaign. It's been three to five days. I didn't meet my success metric. Let me go through this troubleshooting process that Molly taught me to come up with new ideas about how I can fix this current campaign or launch it. It's better. It's troubleshooting and that is very reactive troubleshooting or it's, okay, I've been running this campaign for a while. I've scaled it. It's no longer working. I'm not hitting my success metrics. Let's go back to my troubleshooting process and figure out, can I fix this or do I need to launch a new campaign based off of my findings? So that's really the reactive part of troubleshooting. And for me, this is what I do every morning, five to six days a week, probably now so more like four to five days a week, because all of the accounts that I run are more predictable. ## [24:27 - 27:28]

going through a very simple process. This probably takes me 10, 20, maybe at most 30 minutes if I'm really having to dig deep into campaigns. But this maintenance goes back to remembering your campaign success metrics. So when you launch a campaign, you want to make sure that you have one success metric that judges whether that campaign is a success or a failure. For some of us, it's return on ad spin, cost per acquisition, cost per lead. You want to make sure that you have one metric that you're looking at to judge success or failure. If you don't, it will, you'll have trouble making decisions about whether you should scale, whether you should turn it off, what you should do next. So that's why I recommend picking one number that really judges the success of this campaign. And I use this scale. You guys might have heard me talk about it before, but it's really simple. We call it green, yellow, red. And I'm looking through all of my campaigns and all of my ad sets. And this is my maintenance process. So if that ad set or that campaign is green and for different, you know, for you guys, this is going to mean different things. But essentially, this means that my success metric is in a place that tells me that I should scale. So for example, right now, I showed you guys this on Tuesday, and I'm going to show you more in today's session. This is an account that I just created for a docu series about wine. So brand new ad account that I created last week, you can see that these are our first four campaigns. We're optimizing for leads. And right now our goal, our success metric is to keep these leads under $5 apiece. That is what we know is going to be profitable for us and, you know, where we need to be when it comes to profitability in this funnel. So if I know that I need to keep this under $5, green for me, right, scale, would probably be anything under $4 or anything under $3 and $0.75 would be something that I would scale. Same thing with Ezra. His normal CPA goal is about 60 bucks. So anything under $45, any ad set or campaign, depending on how it's been set up, I'll talk about that here in a bit, that is in the green. That is good to scale. And the reason that I say that is once I get over the $4 mark, I know that this campaign over time is going to fatigue. So if it's over $4 by increasing the budget or whatever I'm going to do to scale as we'll talk about later, it's probably going to quickly push this over to the $5 mark, and I'm not going to get the volume of results that I want.

## [27:28 - 30:30]

So a good way to look at this is if you have 20% to 30% wiggle room left in your cost per lead or cost per acquisition or return on ad spin, before you hit that number that you know you need for your success metric or that you set as your goal, you're really in the green there, and you should increase your budget. So if something's green, so for me, it would be me logging in here. For these campaigns, I've got budget set at the campaign level. So here, this isn't in the yellow, right? This is over $4. This definitely, or I'm sorry, this isn't in the green. This is more in the yellow as I'll talk about in a minute. This is over five bucks, so it's more in the red. But this is a campaign that I just launched two days ago. So this cost per lead is coming down drastically every day. Yesterday, it was 10 bucks. So I'm not doing anything with this campaign yet. This is new. Both of these campaigns have budgets at the ad set level. So I would actually go in at the ad set level and start to make decisions. So if your budget is set at the campaign level, which means you're using CBO, campaign budget optimization, you want to make decisions when it comes to optimization or scale about that campaign at the campaign level because that is how Facebook is optimizing. So for these two campaigns, I would simply be looking at this number here, not the ad level, not the ad set level. I would be looking here in my daily maintenance to make these decisions because these budgets are set at the ad set level. I'm going to be making the decisions here at the ad set level. So I would go through, you can see this one's already at 650 bucks a day. That's because this cost per result has stayed below the $4 mark. I've increased this budget a few times now. And as you see on this slide, I recommend increasing a budget of an ad set or campaign that's green, about 50% every three to five days. This will change depending on the longevity ability of the campaign. So if you were running a campaign that could run for months that doesn't have an end date, you might only want to increase the budget every seven days, maybe 10 days or even 14 days, because we know that when we increase the budget, it's naturally going to decrease return on ad spend. It's naturally going to increase CPA. So by only feeding Facebook that budget every so often versus every three to five days, it won't jack up that CPA or lower that return on ad spend as quickly. Or if you're in a mode like I am here where I only have a few more days to run these ads because we're in the middle of the

## [33:35 - 36:36]

and based off of the success metric that best reflects your campaigns. So make these decisions based off of where you've set your budget. And I said, let me show you, I've already showed you. And then of course, the second aspect of optimization is troubleshooting. We covered that last week, what to do if it's just not working, do not panic. We must dissect the campaign and figure out why it's not working. And I went into great detail about that last week so that you can improve that aspect of the campaign and not completely start from scratch because that's a huge mistake. I see people making their launchy campaign, they run it for three to five days, it doesn't work, and they completely start from scratch. Don't do that. Use the troubleshooting process that I taught you last week. Don't completely start from scratch because even though it was a failure in terms of not meeting your success metrics, you at least bought a bunch of data that should, if you're willing to dissect it, give you some very informed next steps that will help you set up a new campaign that's going to perform a bit better than your last. So when should you troubleshoot when you're not hitting your success metric? And what should you do if you are hitting your success metric? That's really when we get to scale. And there are three ways to scale. So you can see that we're kind of breaking this into three parts. The first part is the building of the campaign, the building of the campaigns as a part of your traffic system. And we spent the first seven to eight weeks of the class on that. And if we don't nail those steps, we're never going to get to scaling. It's why those are so important. We launched the campaign. We then moved to the optimization and troubleshooting portion. Or if everything looks great right off the bat, like it did with the wine campaigns that I showed you, it's time for scale. And there are really three different ways to scale as a media buyer. The first is vertical. And this is what I was discussing earlier about increasing your budgets. This one's super easy. You just have to play nice with Facebook's algorithms. So most people don't realize that it's not wise to go from spending $100 a day to $200 a day the next day, right? Or especially $100 a day to $500 a day or $1,000 a day the next day. That's why I recommend 50% increase in budget every three to five days. Because this gives Facebook the budget. It's kind of like slowly feeding a baby, right? That's kind of how this is. You want to slowly feed Facebook's

## [36:36 - 39:37]

algorithm budget over time so that you don't see a really fast increase in your CPA or really stark decrease in your return on ad spend. A lot of that is happening because we're increasing the budget too quickly. And the reason that happens is that Facebook's currently going through an optimization process for your campaign that's based off of the budget you're spending and the audience that you're targeting. If you 10x that tomorrow, it forces Facebook to show your ads and therefore, you know, because they're needing to spend 10 times more money than they did yesterday to a bunch of people that they're not ready to show the ads to yet because they're just not as qualified as the audiences that they've been showing the ads to in the past that have been converting well. So you're forcing Facebook's optimization process to essentially go outside of its comfort zone too quickly. And that's why you see that CPA job. So when you're vertically scaling, which is very simple, very fast, it's easy to go in and increase those budgets at the ad set level or the campaign level. Make sure that you do it very slowly. The only time that I would increase budgets quickly, like maybe double or triple budgets day over day, is if you were in the middle of a launch and you had, you know, only a very limited amount of time to spend and reach is more important than return on ad spend or CPA. That's the only time that I have just ranked it up and maybe tripled or quadrupled budgets day after day. But if something's working, right, and you know, you want more of it and you're looking to scale vertical scaling and increasing the budget is the first place that I go. And as we discussed, the speed of which you scale that budget really depends on the longevity intention of the campaign. And that's how you should judge how often and by how much you should be increasing those budgets. all by 50 bucks, they all were increased to $1.50. Two to three days later, logged back in. And I don't think any of them are at $1.50 here. Okay, so logged back in, everything was looking good. ## [42:40 - 45:42]

think that is what scale is. They think that there's some trick to increasing budgets that they're missing. But they're really not. It just eventually fatigues, because there's only so many times Facebook can show those ads to that audience that you're targeting. It is natural that that what you are doing will fatigue and that it will not work forever. And that's okay. That is why we have the second type of scaling, which is called horizontal scaling, which is something that I came up with a few years ago. And horizontal scaling is kind of opposite to vertical scaling. It's about taking what's working. So what's working for wine revealed is, you know, targeting the wine market. I mean, this is definitely a market-centric business. There aren't really avatars here. So targeting the wine market, these ads are working. So the offer is working, the copy is working. I mean, everything's working pretty well here. So, you know, and that's where you are when you're ready to scale. Things are working. So it's about taking those assets, the ads, especially, and showing them to more people, right, through new targeting. So, you know, I've got these initial test campaigns for this docuseries. But I hadn't tried no targeting yet. I hadn't tried lookalikes. There are lots of interests I haven't used yet. So now, instead of just increasing the budgets and vertically scaling what's working, another way for me to scale is horizontally. So taking the post IDs of those ads and what's already working and either creating new ad sets or creating new campaigns that target new targeting options that will allow me to reach more people in my market that I haven't reached yet based off of, you know, the interests and what I'm targeting right now that will allow me to scale. And this is honestly one of my favorite ways to scale in the most underrated way to scale. So what other targeting options can I use to find this audience or this avatar? target. This is when I start to look at, hey, do I have about a thousand of, you know, pixel events or pixel fires on my purchase or on my lead? Horizontal targeting is all about taking what's working in terms of the offer and the ads that are working and now just scaling by showing to more people. So this is a little exercise that we did with Boom last year to look at their 2018 spend and say, did you use all of the targeting options available to you? Yes, we targeted our site visitors, email list, buyers, all of our video viewers, our page, you know, fans, Instagram and Facebook, Engagers, local like audiences, but no, Boom didn't use any interest targeting at all in 2018. Well, guess where most of their scale came from this year in 2019? It came from us using the same ads and same offers that they used in 2018, but just a tons, just tons of new interests that allowed us to reach more of their audience that they had never reached before because they, you know, just hadn't found them through the audience or through the targeting that they were using. So this setup will look different again, depending on whether your campaigns are at with budgets at the ad set level or whether you're using CDO with campaigns or excuse me, budgets at the campaign level. But the thought process is the same. So there are really two ways you can do this. If you have budgets set at the ad set level, like you see here, for horizontal scaling, you can simply take the post IDs of the ads that you're already using and create new ad sets with new targeting using those same post IDs. And the reason you can do that is because when you have budget set at the ad set level, you can add new ad sets into those campaigns without throwing off the optimization of the campaign. Now, if you have budgets at the campaign level, you cannot add new ad sets into the campaign without throwing off the optimization and the overall success of the full campaign. So if your budgets are at the campaign level currently, and you want more scale, you want to do more horizontal scaling, you need to create new campaigns using CDO. Really, anytime you want to create a campaign for scale guys, I recommend using CDO. So even though these two test campaigns, they were built for testing and their budgets are at the ad set level, when it was time for scale and after I had proof of concept,

And a reason that I do this and that it

## [1:04:03 - 1:07:06]

works so well is number one, most of these people have already seen ads from you because you've targeted them before, but maybe they just didn't resonate with the hook that you were using before or the hook of the ad that they saw. And if they see brand new ads with maybe new creatives that might catch their attention where the other ones didn't, or a completely new hook that's just much more relevant to them than the ads that they previously saw, that is where a lot of scale can come from, especially after campaigns that you're running currently that you're relying on start to fatigue. This is a great way to continue to still communicate from that audience to that audience and essentially refresh what has already fatigued. And this really involves, I mean, sometimes guys, it's about creating new hooks and therefore new creative and copy every four to 30, 60 days. It depends on the volume of traffic that you're running. There are some brands that are running a low volume of traffic that might only need to create fresh campaigns every two, three, four months because things just don't fatigue that quickly. They don't need to be scaled that much, but there are some companies like boom, where we're scaling so quickly and running at such a high volume and campaigns are fatiguing so quickly that new stuff needs to be launched every week. And that's just part of the process. And you will start to notice the cadence of how quickly your campaigns fatigue based off how quickly you're scaling them. And therefore, that is really the cadence you should follow in terms of how often you should be creating new ad creative and new copy to replace those campaigns that have fatigued. So, exponential scaling is not just about opening up to new locations or creating new hooks. It's also the creation of new creative and copy to reflect those new hooks that we're trying to see if these audiences will resonate with. I discussed this at the beginning and this is something that Ezra is really strong about with boom, but the introduction of new products. That's something that John and I have worked a lot on. We've got trained by traffic person, but we've also launched team traffic and we've got smart traffic live. The reason that we're launching new products is we want to further serve you guys and we love it, but we also know that will increase the customer value and that we're now launching products at different price points that are going to help people at every step in the customer journey. So, simply by creating new products, boom, again, is great at this. By just creating new products, it can help you not only further monetize existing customers,

## [1:07:06 - 1:10:12]

which creates an opportunity for very profitable ad campaigns, but it also gives you new products that might work to cold traffic or might work to cold audiences that you've tried to reach before that just didn't resonate with the offer or the product that you were trying to sell. So, by creating new products, whether it's front end or even more expensive back end products, by releasing new products at least once a year, that's going to give you something fresh to talk about and really allows for that exponential scale. And then last but not least, John is, okay, something's working, right? It's working on Facebook. This offer is resonating this hook, this copy are creative. If people here are liking it, those humans are probably on other traffic platforms or people like those humans are probably on other traffic platforms like YouTube or Google. So, how can we take what we're doing here and horizontally scale, exponentially scale over to these other traffic platforms? Once you have something working on Facebook, how can you scale it out to these other traffic platforms to really get that exponential scale? If it's working on one, yes, it will need to be tweaked because every traffic platform works differently and consumers use traffic platforms differently. So, it will need to be tweaked. You can't just take a Facebook campaign and plop it into Google. But there is a high likelihood that if positioned in the correct way and if tweaked in the correct way, what you are doing in theory on Facebook is going to work on these other traffic platforms that will allow you to reach parts of your market that you just haven't been able to reach on Facebook. Maybe they just haven't identified as a part of your market on Facebook. Maybe they just don't spend a lot of time on Facebook. Maybe they are spending a lot of time on YouTube or on another traffic platform. So, another way to exponentially scale is by finding other traffic platforms that will allow you to reach this audience. So, really the three big ways to scale guys. First is vertical increase in the budget. Second is horizontal finding more people that are a part of your market on the traffic platform that you're using and using interests and lookalikes and open audiences that you haven't used before for horizontal scale. And then third is exponential, which is everything that I just covered in the last five to 10 minutes.
